key_facts.Custody: Bridge smart contracts and router liquidity

intent.a: Anyswap is best researched today as the legacy name for Multichain, a cross-chain router and bridge system. Users still search "Anyswap bridge" when trying to understand old routes, token contracts, or transactions, but current usage should be approached carefully because Multichain experienced major operational and security failures in 2023. The high-value query is not just how to bridge; it is whether a route is live, supported, and safe for a specific token. Before interacting with any Anyswap or Multichain contract, users should verify the official domain, route status, explorer activity, token issuer guidance, and whether the destination token still has reliable redemption or liquidity.

Anyswap and Multichain fees historically depended on the token route, source chain gas, destination chain gas, liquidity, and whether the bridge used a mint/burn or liquidity-pool design. Speed could vary from minutes to much longer if liquidity, confirmations, or route infrastructure were constrained. For legacy transactions, users should inspect the transaction hash on both source and destination explorers and compare it with the router's status page if available. For new transfers, the safer assumption is that fees and time estimates from old articles may be stale. A modern bridge decision should prioritize live route health and issuer-supported canonical bridges.

ANY was associated with Anyswap, while MULTI became the token associated with Multichain after the rebrand. Searchers often want to know whether an old ANY token, exchange balance, or wallet holding maps to MULTI. That question should be answered through official token migration records, exchange announcements, and current market pages rather than assumptions from ticker similarity. A token may remain visible on-chain even when exchange support, governance utility, or liquidity has changed. Users should verify token contract addresses, current exchange handling, and whether any migration route is still open before buying, selling, or transferring either asset.

Anyswap and Multichain became known for broad cross-chain support across EVM and non-EVM ecosystems, but historical support is not the same as safe current support. A route can appear in an old UI, blog post, or explorer while liquidity, issuer backing, or bridge operations are impaired. Users should check the exact asset, source chain, destination chain, and token issuer recommendation. For blue-chip assets, canonical bridges or issuer-native transfers are usually easier to verify. For long-tail assets, the key risk is not only failed transfer; it is receiving a bridged token that has weak liquidity or no trusted redemption path.

compare.title: Anyswap vs modern canonical bridges
rows: Depends on chain security and bridge design
Check route, contract, and status carefully
Check official chain or token issuer docs

faq.q: Is Anyswap the same as Multichain?
faq.a: Anyswap rebranded into Multichain, so many old Anyswap searches refer to the Multichain bridge and router ecosystem. The names are connected, but users should treat old Anyswap guides as historical. Current safety, route status, token contracts, and liquidity need fresh verification before any transaction.

faq.q: Can I still use Anyswap bridge?
faq.a: Users should be extremely cautious about using Anyswap or Multichain routes. The project suffered serious operational and security issues, and route availability may not imply safe redemption. Before sending funds, verify official status, token issuer guidance, recent successful transactions, liquidity, and whether a safer canonical bridge exists.

faq.q: What happened to the ANY token?
faq.a: ANY was the earlier token associated with Anyswap before Multichain branding and token changes. Holders should verify any migration or exchange handling through official announcements and market listings. A visible on-chain token balance does not prove that a migration is active, that liquidity is deep, or that exchanges support deposits.

faq.q: Why is my Anyswap transaction pending?
faq.a: A pending or incomplete Anyswap transaction can come from source-chain confirmation delay, bridge liquidity issues, router problems, destination-chain congestion, or a route that is no longer operating normally. Users should save the transaction hash, check both chain explorers, and avoid sending repeated transfers until the route status is understood.

Not necessarily. A bridged token from Anyswap or Multichain may be a representation issued by the bridge rather than the canonical token for the destination chain. Canonical status depends on the token issuer and chain ecosystem. Users should verify with the token project's official docs before depositing bridged assets into exchanges or protocols.

faq.q: What is the safest alternative to Anyswap?
faq.a: The safer alternative is usually the official bridge recommended by the chain or token issuer, such as a network bridge, issuer-supported mint-and-burn system, or well-maintained current bridge with transparent risk disclosures. The best choice depends on the exact asset and chains. A route should be judged by live support, liquidity, and audit history.
